== Notes for [[Item:Q2212|Q2212]] ==

RESEARCH NOTES:
<p>Queen of Maurya Empire</p>
<p>{geni:about_me}</p>
<p>=Subhadrangi=</p>
<p>Empress consort of the Maurya Empire </p>
<p>Spouse</p>
<p>Bindusara </p>
<p>Issue</p>
<p>Ashoka</p>
<p>Vitashoka</p>
<p>Dynasty Maurya </p>
<p>Religion Ajivika </p>
<p>Subhadrangi (also known as Dharma or Janapadakalyani) was, according to Buddhist sources, a wife of the Mauryan emperor, Bindusara and the mother of his successor, Ashoka. The Ashokavadana states that Subhadrangi was the daughter of a Brahmin from the city of Champa. The legends state that palace politics kept her away from Bindusara and when she finally gained access to him and bore him a son, she is said to have exclaimed, "I am now without sorrow", which led to the child being named Ashoka. The name of her second son, Vitashoka (meaning sorrow terminated) has a similar provenance.[1]:332[2]
<p>The Divyavadana calls her Dharma while the Vamsatthapakasini, a 10th-century commentary on the Mahavamsa,[1]:321 names her as Janapadakalyani.[1]:332</p>
<p>In popular culture[edit] Subhadrangi's role is played by Pallavi Subhash in the TV series, Chakravartin Ashoka Samrat[3] She was portrayed by Subhashini Ali in the 2001 film Aśoka.</p>
<p>References[edit]</p>
<p>1.^ Jump up to: a b c Singh, Upinder (2008). A history of ancient and early medieval India : from the Stone Age to the 12th century. New Delhi: Pearson Education. pp. 321–332. ISBN 9788131711200. Retrieved 8 September 2015. 2.Jump up ^ Thapar, Romila (1961). Aśoka and the decline of the Mauryas (2nd ed.). New Delhi: Oxford University Press. p. 21. Retrieved 8 September 2015. 3.Jump up ^ Playing onscreen mother was a challenge: Pallavi Subhash, IBN Live, 31 January 2015
<p>---------------------------</p>
<p>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brahmin</p>
<p>--------------------</p>
<p>'''Subhadrangi'''</p>
<p>Subhadrangi (also known as Dharma or Janapadakalyani) was, according to Buddhist sources, a wife of the Mauryan emperor, Bindusara and the mother of his successor, Ashoka. The Ashokavadana states that Subhadrangi was the daughter of a Brahmin from the city of Champa. The legends state that palace politics kept her away from Bindusara and when she finally gained access to him and bore him a son, she is said to have exclaimed, "I am now without sorrow", which led to the child being named Ashoka. The name of her second son, Vitashoka (meaning sorrow terminated) has a similar provenance.
<p>The Divyavadana calls her Dharma while the Vamsatthapakasini, a 10th-century commentary on the Mahavamsa, names her as Janapadakalyani.</p>
<p>'''Source : '''</p>
<p>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Subhadrangi</p>
<p>SOURCE NOTES:</p>
<p>http://www.american-pictures.com/genealogy/persons/per04874.htm#0</p>
<p>-- MERGED NOTE ------------</p>
<p>{geni:about_me}</p>
<p>=Subhadrangi=</p>
<p>Empress consort of the Maurya Empire </p>
<p>Spouse</p>
<p>Bindusara </p>
<p>Issue</p>
<p>Ashoka</p>
<p>Vitashoka</p>
<p>Dynasty Maurya </p>
<p>Religion Ajivika </p>
<p>Subhadrangi (also known as Dharma or Janapadakalyani) was, according to Buddhist sources, a wife of the Mauryan emperor, Bindusara and the mother of his successor, Ashoka. The Ashokavadana states that Subhadrangi was the daughter of a Brahmin from the city of Champa. The legends state that palace politics kept her away from Bindusara and when she finally gained access to him and bore him a son, she is said to have exclaimed, "I am now without sorrow", which led to the child being named Ashoka. The name of her second son, Vitashoka (meaning sorrow terminated) has a similar provenance.[1]:332[2]
<p>The Divyavadana calls her Dharma while the Vamsatthapakasini, a 10th-century commentary on the Mahavamsa,[1]:321 names her as Janapadakalyani.[1]:332</p>
<p>In popular culture[edit] Subhadrangi's role is played by Pallavi Subhash in the TV series, Chakravartin Ashoka Samrat[3] She was portrayed by Subhashini Ali in the 2001 film Aśoka.</p>
<p>References[edit]</p>
<p>1.^ Jump up to: a b c Singh, Upinder (2008). A history of ancient and early medieval India : from the Stone Age to the 12th century. New Delhi: Pearson Education. pp. 321–332. ISBN 9788131711200. Retrieved 8 September 2015. 2.Jump up ^ Thapar, Romila (1961). Aśoka and the decline of the Mauryas (2nd ed.). New Delhi: Oxford University Press. p. 21. Retrieved 8 September 2015. 3.Jump up ^ Playing onscreen mother was a challenge: Pallavi Subhash, IBN Live, 31 January 2015
<p>---------------------------</p>
<p>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brahmin</p>
<p>--------------------</p>
<p>'''Subhadrangi'''</p>
<p>Subhadrangi (also known as Dharma or Janapadakalyani) was, according to Buddhist sources, a wife of the Mauryan emperor, Bindusara and the mother of his successor, Ashoka. The Ashokavadana states that Subhadrangi was the daughter of a Brahmin from the city of Champa. The legends state that palace politics kept her away from Bindusara and when she finally gained access to him and bore him a son, she is said to have exclaimed, "I am now without sorrow", which led to the child being named Ashoka. The name of her second son, Vitashoka (meaning sorrow terminated) has a similar provenance.
<p>The Divyavadana calls her Dharma while the Vamsatthapakasini, a 10th-century commentary on the Mahavamsa, names her as Janapadakalyani.</p>
<p>'''Source : '''</p>
<p>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Subhadrangi</p>
<p>SOURCE NOTES:</p>
<p>http://www.american-pictures.com/genealogy/persons/per04874.htm#0</p>
<p>RESEARCH NOTES:</p>
<p>Queen of Maurya Empire</p>

[[Category:GEDCOM Notes Pages]][[Category:Pages]]
